channel.title: Latest business news
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026, Australian Broadcasting Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

item.title: Touring musicians rethink careers as fuel prices bite
item.description: Adele Oliver admits being a country music artist has "never been a great career choice", but she says things are worse than ever, with her last gig costing her money due to the escalating price of fuel.
item.dc:creator: Mietta Adams

media:group.media:description: Adele Oliver is looking for other jobs outside music while fuel prices stay high.

item.title: Fight over gas taxes intensifies as major project expansion approved
item.description: Natural gas taxes are back in the fray after the expansion of Chevron's Gorgon project off WA's north-west coast received environmental approval amid a Senate inquiry into how resources are taxed.
item.dc:creator: Cason Ho

media:group.media:description: Part of Chevron's Gorgon project on Barrow Island, off WA's coast.

item.title: Profits shrivel as yellow tail winemaker posts $5.5 million loss
item.description: The maker of one of Australia's best-known wine brands, yellow tail, says rising costs and global factors such as tariffs are behind the sharp drop in profits.
item.dc:creator: Emily Doak

media:group.media:description: Family-owned Casella Wines's financial results have swung from an $18 million profit in 2024 to a $5.5 million loss in 2025.

item.title: Price of groceries to increase within weeks due to war in Iran
item.description: Farmers ask for a 30-cent-per-litre price rise for milk as fuel and fertiliser prices surge.
item.dc:creator: Jane McNaughton

media:group.media:description: Australian food prices are set to increase as the Middle East war continues.

item.title: NT ranked last for illicit tobacco laws in new report
item.description: Smoking is one of the leading causes of death in the Northern Territory. Now, the jurisdiction has been ranked the weakest in the country for how it tackles cheap, illicit tobacco.
item.dc:creator: Olivana Lathouris

media:group.media:description: Laura Hunter says the Northern Territory was ranked last in the country for how it tackles illicit tobacco.

item.title: The real population of this remote opal town is anyone's guess
item.description: As the 2026 census approaches, residents in Lightning Ridge fear inaccurate calculations of their population will affect the provision of essential services.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Blandin de Chalain

media:group.media:description: Residents of Lightning Ridge say they have been negatively impacted by inaccurate population data.

item.title: Communities 'in the middle of nowhere' are scared they'll run out of fuel
item.description: Remote communities are calling for assurance on fuel security in the face of potential shortages, along with financial assistance as already-high fuel and food prices continue to climb.Â
item.dc:creator: Jane Bardon

media:group.media:description: Alastair King wants the federal government's help to make sure grocery stores in remote communities remain stocked.

item.title: The skis being made from three types of Australian-grown timber
item.description: A couple in the NSW Snowy Mountains makes skis entirely from Australian-grown timber, though manufacturing for the snow industry in a "summer country" comes with its share of challenges.
item.dc:creator: Isla Evans and Floss Adams

media:group.media:description: Steph and Jonathan Paige make skis in a workshop in Jindabyne.

item.title: Automation is growing in Australia's mining industry â€” but what are the costs?
item.description: At Australia's largest gold mine, drivers are more likely to be found in a control room than a cabin, but researchers and unions say jobs and safety remain significant concerns.
item.dc:creator: Neve Brissenden

media:group.media:description: Newmont's gold mine in Boddington is semi-autonomous.

item.title: Couple says loophole allowing theft from cars shipped interstate
item.description: Elihu and Amanda Del Valle say thousands of dollars worth of their belongings were stolen from the boot of their car when it was shipped from Melbourne to Perth, but police aren't able to investigate.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kristanto

media:group.media:description: Elihu Del Valle says it is not clear which jurisdiction the theft occurred in.Â

item.title: Authors turn to self-publishing as big publishers look to cut costs
item.description: Is your dream to become the next JK Rowling or Stephanie Meyer? A new wave of writers is turning to self-publishing to turn fantasy into reality.
item.dc:creator: Gavin McGrath

media:group.media:description: Fantasy and other speculative fiction genres have exploded in popularity since 2020.

item.title: Al Kuwait ships cattle out of Darwin after weeks anchored near Fremantle
item.description: Anchored off Fremantle for weeks because the war in Iran had stopped the live sheep trade to the Middle East, the world's largest live export vessel has ventured north to Darwin.Â
item.dc:creator: Matt Brann

media:group.media:description: MV Al Kuwait, in Darwin Port, has the capacity to carry 20,000 cattle, 75,000 sheep, or a combination of both.

item.title: Calls for more earthquake monitors after record quake near gold mine
item.description: The magnitude 4.5-earthquake near Orange was logged as the region's largest on record, but only thanks to public monitoring data far away from the epicentre.
item.dc:creator: Lani Oataway

media:group.media:description: Dave Cowan says his Forest Reefs home felt like it moved "four inches" during the record-breaking earthquake near Orange.

item.title: Octopus species mysteriously disappears from Spencer Gulf
item.description: The once-abundant pale octopus has not been caught in lower Spencer Gulf for more than a year, and what happened to the species remains a mystery.
item.dc:creator: Emma Pedler

media:group.media:description: Leon Van Weenen used to catch 350kg of pale octopus a month.

item.title: 'She was us': History behind Joy, the first sex worker statue
item.description: During her short residency in Sydney, the statue of sex worker Joy had many detractors and was even vandalised. Thirty years later, she has returned, with her supporters hoping she will dismantle stereotypes that still plague the industry.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Michie

media:group.media:description: Joy the bronze statue

item.title: NT councils flag cuts to street lights, pool hours as result of power prices
item.description: Power price hikes are expected to be passed onto local councils by the Northern Territory government in the coming month, with Darwin's lord mayor warning some community services could be cut as a result.
item.dc:creator: Matt Garrick

media:group.media:description: Darwin may have to cut access to some local government services, like library trading hours, as a result of the program.

item.title: Flood-damaged roads cause 'catastrophic' impacts for NT businesses
item.description: Workers in road-reliant industries are calling on the federal government to provide urgent funding to repair the NT's road network, which was severely damaged by floods.
item.dc:creator: Tilda Colling

media:group.media:description: The road to regional NT tourism businesses, Daly River Barra Resort and Woolianna on the Daly, was severely damaged by the recent string of wet weather events.

item.title: Producer backs pitch for major film studio in Port Kembla
item.description: As the New South Wales government searches for a site for a second Sydney film studio, one of Australia's most prominent producers suggests a regional location is the ideal fit. Â
item.dc:creator: Brooke Chandler

media:group.media:description: Film advocates believe a film studio should be built in Port Kembla.

item.title: New scooters and e-bikes have arrived in Canberra. Here's what that means
item.description: With Neuron's orange scooters having left Canberra, Lime has introduced a fleet of green scooters and e-bikes across the capital in partnership with the ACT government.Â
item.dc:creator: Niki Burnside

media:group.media:description: Lime e-bikes and scooters have started operating in Canberra.

item.title: An executive at FIFA's new betting partner settled an insider-trading lawsuit
item.description: The backstory of the prediction market industry's newest entrant has done little to calm concerns as it partners with several giants of sports broadcasting.
item.dc:creator: Julian Fell

item.title: How lawyers are using AI, and financial planners aren't far behind
item.description: Businesses say they are using AI to speed up service delivery and improve the customer experience, but economists warn this may be the "calm before the storm".
item.dc:creator: David Taylor

media:group.media:description: Businesses say AI is helping them speed up service delivery.

item.title: Fishers deploy 'ninja tactics' to outsmart fish-stealing seals
item.description: Commercial fishers question their future as they compete with ravenous long-nosed seals that continue to rip man-sized holes in fish nets in South Australia's Coorong.
item.dc:creator: Becc Bird and Caroline Horn

media:group.media:description: Long-nosed fur seals are protected by state and Commonwealth legislation.

item.title: Labor considering ways to spare new homes from capital gains changes
item.description: The possible budget centrepiece is likely to be finalised much closer to the May deadline than usual this year given global uncertainty, but progress has been made in recent weeks.
item.dc:creator: Tom Crowley

media:group.media:description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has flagged "intergenerational fairness" as a reason for tax reform in the upcoming budget.

item.title: Oil prices plunge after news Strait of Hormuz to open
item.description: Oil prices plunged more than 10 per cent after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz would be "completely open" but shipping giants are approaching the situation with caution.
item.dc:creator: Anna Cox

media:group.media:description: A crude oil ship off the coast of the US.

item.title: Companies and directors fined $7m over payday lending scheme
item.description: The fines were issued against an operation that breached credit rules and generated more than $91 million in fees and charges.
item.dc:creator: Liam Walsh

media:group.media:description: The Federal Court has ordered Cigno Australia to pay $3 million.
